steps.title: Find the gesture (pencil)
steps.description: Make sure you're in pencil mode. Lightly sketch a single curving line for the trunk's gesture — olives lean, twist, and rarely run straight up. Capture that lean before any detail.

steps.title: Block the pot (pencil)
steps.description: Still in pencil, at the bottom of the trunk, sketch a small tapered pot — narrower at the base than the rim. Add a shallow ellipse for the rim opening.

steps.title: Block the canopy (pencil)
steps.description: At the top of the trunk, sketch a loose, lumpy cloud-like outline for the foliage. Olive canopies are uneven and feathery — let the outline dip in and out rather than rounding into a smooth ball.

steps.title: Add the trunk thickness (pencil)
steps.description: Around your gesture line, lightly sketch two roughly parallel lines giving the trunk its width. A slight taper toward the canopy looks more natural than a uniform cylinder.

steps.title: Switch to ink and trace the pot and trunk
steps.description: Switch to ink mode. Trace the pot's tapered sides and rim, then continue up the trunk in confident strokes. Where trunk meets pot rim, land the lines cleanly at the junction.

steps.title: Ink the canopy outline (ink)
steps.description: Still in ink, trace the outer edge of the foliage as an irregular wandering line — small bumps and dips. Inside it, scatter a handful of small curved marks suggesting clusters of leaves, but don't draw every one.

steps.title: Add bark texture (ink)
steps.description: On the trunk, add two or three short curving marks following its lean. Those marks suggest gnarled olive bark — a few are plenty.

steps.title: Clean up
steps.description: Switch to the eraser and remove any pencil guide lines that distract from the ink. A faint gesture line behind the trunk can stay if it feels like nice construction.
